rules.name: Residential Solar + Storage
rules.reasoning: Adding battery storage to a residential solar system provides backup power during outages and can increase self-consumption of your solar energy. Instead of exporting excess solar to the grid during the day and buying it back at night, you store it and use it yourself. This is especially valuable in areas with low net metering rates or time-of-use billing. A single residential battery (10-16 kWh) covers essential loads for 8-12 hours or whole-home backup for 4-6 hours. The battery connects through a hybrid inverter that manages solar, battery, and grid power automatically.
tips: If your primary goal is cost savings, solar-only has a faster payback. Add storage if backup power or self-consumption is important to you
Essential loads backup (lights, fridge, internet, medical devices) needs only 5-10 kWh - the smallest and cheapest battery option
Whole-home backup including HVAC typically needs 15-30 kWh depending on your system and climate
LFP (lithium iron phosphate) batteries are the current standard for residential - safer, longer-lasting, and more stable than NMC chemistry
A hybrid inverter replaces your solar inverter and manages both solar and battery, so you are not paying for two separate inverters
Check if your utility offers time-of-use rates - storing cheap daytime solar and using it during expensive evening peak hours improves ROI significantly

rules.name: Residential Solar Only
rules.reasoning: A grid-tied solar-only system is the simplest and most cost-effective way to reduce your electricity bills. Without a battery, your system exports excess power to the grid during the day and you draw from the grid at night. In areas with strong net metering policies, the grid effectively acts as a free battery. Solar-only systems have the fastest payback period (typically 5-8 years) because you avoid the cost of battery storage. The system connects through a string inverter or microinverters and requires minimal maintenance beyond occasional panel cleaning.
tips: Check your utility's net metering policy before installing - full retail credit makes solar-only highly attractive
Microinverters (like Enphase IQ8) provide panel-level optimization and monitoring, best for roofs with partial shading
String inverters with optimizers (like SolarEdge) offer a good middle ground on cost and performance
Size your system to offset 80-100% of your annual electricity consumption for the best economics
South-facing roof orientation is ideal but east/west facing can still work well with modern high-efficiency panels
The 30% federal ITC reduces your net cost significantly - available through 2032

rules.name: Residential Solar Only (Canada)
rules.reasoning: A grid-tied solar-only system is the simplest and most cost-effective way to reduce your electricity bills. Without a battery, your system exports excess power to the grid during the day and you draw from the grid at night. Canadian net metering policies vary by province but are generally favorable, with many provinces offering full retail credit. Solar-only systems have the fastest payback period. The federal Clean Energy Investment Tax Credit and provincial rebate programs can significantly reduce your net cost.
tips: Check your province's net metering policy - BC, Ontario, and Alberta all have programs but terms differ
The federal Clean Energy Investment Tax Credit (up to 30%) can significantly reduce system cost
BC residents should check BC Hydro net metering and CleanBC programs for additional incentives
Ontario residents may qualify for programs from the IESO and local utility incentives
CCA Class 43.1/43.2 accelerated depreciation is available for business-owned solar installations
Canadian solar production is lower in winter months - size your system based on annual production, not summer peak

rules.name: Commercial Solar + Storage
rules.reasoning: Commercial buildings with high demand charges benefit enormously from solar + storage. Solar reduces daytime energy consumption, and the battery targets demand charge reduction by discharging during your peak usage periods. Many commercial customers pay $10-20 per kW of peak demand per month - a single 100 kW spike for 15 minutes can add $1,000-2,000 to your monthly bill. A well-sized battery can shave 30-50% off demand charges. Combined with the 30% ITC and MACRS depreciation, the effective cost reduction for commercial solar + storage can reach 50-60%.
tips: Get 12 months of interval data from your utility to understand your demand profile before sizing
Battery sizing for demand charge reduction is driven by your peak demand (kW), not energy consumption (kWh)
The 30% ITC + 5-year MACRS depreciation can reduce effective system cost by 50-60%
Three-phase string inverters (SMA, Fronius) are the standard for commercial rooftop
Consider a power purchase agreement (PPA) if capital expenditure is a constraint - you pay per kWh with no upfront cost
Flat commercial roofs are ideal for ballasted racking systems that require no roof penetrations

rules.name: Agricultural Solar
rules.reasoning: Agricultural operations have unique advantages for solar: large open land for ground-mount arrays, metal buildings ideal for roof mounting, high daytime energy loads (irrigation, cooling, processing), and access to USDA REAP grants that can cover 25-50% of project cost on top of the 30% federal ITC. Ground-mount arrays on agricultural land can be designed to allow farming activity underneath (agrivoltaics). Metal roof systems with standing seam clamps are the fastest and cheapest installation method.
tips: USDA REAP grants (25% grant + 25% loan guarantee) stack with the 30% ITC for total incentive coverage of 55-80%
Ground-mount trackers increase production by 15-25% compared to fixed-tilt, worth it in most locations
Metal roof standing seam clamps require zero penetrations - no leaks, fast installation
Size for your irrigation season peak load to offset the most expensive electricity months
Agrivoltaics (raised panels allowing crops or grazing underneath) can provide dual income from the same land
Check your co-op or utility for specific agricultural net metering or time-of-use rates

rules.name: Agricultural Solar (Canada)
rules.reasoning: Agricultural operations in Canada have strong advantages for solar: large open land for ground-mount arrays, metal buildings ideal for roof mounting, and high seasonal energy loads. The federal Clean Energy Investment Tax Credit and CCA Class 43.1/43.2 accelerated depreciation can significantly reduce net cost. Some provinces offer additional agricultural energy programs. Ground-mount arrays in Canadian climates benefit from higher albedo (snow reflection) in winter months.
tips: The federal Clean Energy Investment Tax Credit (up to 30%) is available for agricultural solar installations
CCA Class 43.1/43.2 accelerated depreciation substantially reduces the effective cost for farm businesses
Ground-mount trackers increase production by 15-25% and are especially valuable at Canadian latitudes
Snow reflection (albedo effect) can boost winter production by 10-15% with bifacial panels
Check your provincial agricultural ministry for on-farm energy programs and grants
Alberta and Saskatchewan have strong solar resources despite their northern latitude

rules.name: Community Microgrid
rules.reasoning: Community microgrids provide resilient power for critical facilities (hospitals, emergency services, shelters, water treatment) that must remain operational during grid outages. A microgrid combines solar, battery storage, and a microgrid controller that can disconnect from the main grid (islanding) and operate autonomously. For community buildings, the IRA Direct Pay provision allows tax-exempt entities (municipalities, schools, hospitals) to receive the 30% ITC as a direct cash payment instead of a tax credit.
tips: IRA Direct Pay (elective pay) lets tax-exempt entities claim the 30% ITC as a direct cash payment
Microgrid controllers (like Schneider EcoStruxure or Siemens SICAM) manage islanding and grid reconnection automatically
Size the battery for critical loads during the longest expected outage (typically 24-72 hours)
Include a generator as backup for extended outages beyond battery capacity
Community resilience projects often qualify for FEMA BRIC grants in addition to energy incentives
Start with a critical loads analysis to determine which circuits must stay powered during an outage

rules.name: Remote / Off-Grid System
rules.reasoning: Remote and off-grid applications require a self-contained power system that can operate independently. The combination of solar + battery storage + a backup generator provides reliable 24/7 power without grid connection. Modern off-grid systems use inverter/chargers that manage all power sources automatically. Sizing is critical: you must generate and store enough energy to cover your consumption during the worst solar month (typically winter). LFP batteries are strongly preferred for off-grid because they handle daily deep cycling without degradation.
tips: Size solar for the worst production month (December/January), not the annual average
Battery capacity should cover 2-3 days of autonomy (days of full load without solar)
LFP batteries are essential for off-grid - they handle thousands of deep cycles without degradation
Include a backup generator for extended cloudy periods and unexpected demand
Remote BC and northern Canadian communities can access CleanBC diesel displacement funding
Monitor your system remotely via cellular modem - off-grid systems need proactive maintenance

rules.name: Standalone Battery Storage
rules.reasoning: Installing battery storage without solar is increasingly common for backup power, demand charge reduction, and time-of-use arbitrage. Grid-connected batteries charge from the grid during off-peak hours (cheap electricity) and discharge during on-peak hours (expensive electricity). For commercial customers, this can reduce demand charges by 30-50%. For residential customers in areas with poor net metering, a battery can store cheap nighttime electricity for daytime use. Standalone storage qualifies for the 30% ITC as of the Inflation Reduction Act.
tips: Standalone storage now qualifies for the 30% ITC under the Inflation Reduction Act - a major cost reduction
For demand charge reduction, size the battery's power (kW) to your target peak reduction, not capacity (kWh)
AC-coupled batteries (like Enphase IQ Battery) can retrofit into any existing electrical system
If you plan to add solar later, choose a battery and inverter that support future solar integration
Time-of-use arbitrage works best where on-peak and off-peak rates differ by more than $0.10/kWh
Commercial customers should analyze 12 months of demand data before sizing

rules.name: Community / Municipal Solar
rules.reasoning: Community and municipal solar projects have a unique advantage: the IRA Direct Pay provision allows tax-exempt entities (municipalities, school districts, hospitals, nonprofits) to receive the 30% ITC as a direct cash payment instead of a tax credit. This eliminates the need for tax equity partners or third-party ownership structures. Community buildings with consistent daytime load (schools, libraries, municipal offices) are ideal for solar because generation and consumption align.
tips: IRA Direct Pay lets tax-exempt entities claim the 30% ITC as a direct check from the IRS
Meeting prevailing wage and apprenticeship requirements increases the ITC from 6% to 30%
Issue an RFP specifying system size, O&M requirements, and performance guarantees
Community solar programs allow residents without suitable roofs to subscribe to a shared array
School districts should consider summer construction timelines when parking lots and campuses are empty
Check state-level REAP grants and utility rebates that stack on top of IRA Direct Pay

rules.name: General Renewables Recommendation
rules.reasoning: Based on your inputs, here is a general starting point. Solar PV is the most cost-effective renewable energy investment for most buildings, with typical payback periods of 5-10 years and 25+ year system life. Adding battery storage increases cost but provides backup power and can improve economics in areas with demand charges or time-of-use rates. The 30% federal ITC significantly reduces net cost and is available through 2032. Start by getting 3 quotes from local installers to understand site-specific costs.
tips: Get 3 quotes from different installers to compare pricing and system designs
The 30% federal ITC is available through 2032 for solar and standalone storage
Check your state and utility for additional incentives that stack with the federal ITC
Size your system based on your actual electricity consumption, not a generic estimate
Choose Tier 1 bankable panel manufacturers for the best warranty protection
Consider your future electricity needs (EV charging, heat pumps) when sizing your system

rules.name: General Renewables Recommendation (Canada)
rules.reasoning: Based on your inputs, here is a general starting point. Solar PV is a strong investment in Canada, with typical payback periods of 8-12 years depending on your province and electricity rates. Adding battery storage provides backup power and can improve economics with time-of-use billing. The federal Clean Energy Investment Tax Credit and CCA Class 43.1/43.2 accelerated depreciation significantly reduce net cost. Provincial programs from BC Hydro, Hydro-Quebec, and Ontario utilities can further reduce costs. Start by getting 3 quotes from local installers.
tips: Get 3 quotes from different installers to compare pricing and system designs
The federal Clean Energy Investment Tax Credit (up to 30%) is available for clean energy installations
Check your provincial utility for net metering programs and additional rebates
CCA Class 43.1/43.2 accelerated depreciation is available for business installations
BC, Ontario, and Quebec have the most developed incentive programs for renewables
Consider your future electricity needs (EV charging, heat pumps) when sizing your system

rules.name: Residential Level 2 Charging
rules.reasoning: For home charging, a Level 2 charger at 240V provides the best balance of speed, cost, and simplicity. Most EVs fully charge overnight on a Level 2 charger, adding 40-80 km of range per hour. This is the most cost-effective charging scenario since you are using your existing home electrical service and charging during off-peak hours when electricity is cheapest.
tips: Check if your electrical panel has a spare 50A or 60A slot before purchasing a charger
A 48A charger on a 60A breaker is the sweet spot for most homes
If you have two EVs, look for chargers with power sharing to avoid needing two separate circuits
Many utilities offer time-of-use rates with cheaper electricity at night
If you rent, a portable 240V charger with a NEMA 14-50 plug lets you take it with you when you move

rules.name: Workplace Level 2 Charging
rules.reasoning: Workplace charging is ideal for Level 2 because employees park for 4-8 hours, providing plenty of time for even a standard charger to fully replenish a day's driving. At 11.5 kW, an employee driving 60 km to work would fully recover that energy in about 1.5 hours, leaving the remaining dwell time as buffer.
tips: Start with a pilot of 4-8 ports to gauge demand before committing to a large deployment
Load management is essential for 4+ ports - it can reduce your electrical infrastructure costs by 40-60%
Choose OCPP-compatible chargers to avoid being locked into one network provider's fees
Check federal and provincial/state incentives - workplace charging incentives can cover 50-80% of costs
Dual-port chargers cut installation costs by sharing one circuit between two ports

rules.name: Fleet Depot Overnight Charging
rules.reasoning: Fleet depots with overnight dwell times are the most cost-effective EV charging scenario. With 8-12 hours of charging time, you can use Level 2 chargers with aggressive load management to minimize electrical infrastructure. Load management is critical: 25 chargers with load management might only need a 200A service, versus 1,500A without it.
tips: Daisy-chain chargers (like Siemens VersiCharge XL) to minimize conduit runs and installation cost
Schedule charging to start at 10 PM or later for off-peak electricity rates
Install sub-metering to track energy costs per vehicle for fleet TCO analysis
Plan for 100% fleet electrification from the start in your infrastructure design
Load management is not optional for fleet - it is the single most impactful cost reduction tool

rules.name: Retail / Public DC Fast Charging
rules.reasoning: Short customer visits require DC Fast Charging. At 50-150 kW, drivers can add 100-300 km of range in 15-30 minutes. DCFC costs 10-30x more than Level 2, but it generates revenue and drives foot traffic.
tips: Start with 2 DC fast chargers and add more based on utilization data
50-75 kW chargers are significantly cheaper to install than 150+ kW and sufficient for most retail scenarios
NEVI grants can cover up to 80% of highway corridor DCFC costs
Demand charges can be your biggest ongoing cost - discuss rate options with your utility
Consider a compact charger (like Tritium RTM 75) if space is limited

rules.name: Hotel / Hospitality Level 2 Charging
rules.reasoning: Hotels are an excellent Level 2 use case because guests park overnight, providing ample time for full charging. EV drivers actively seek hotels with chargers and will pay a premium or choose your property over a competitor without charging.
tips: Promote EV charging on your booking platforms - EV drivers filter for hotels with charging
Start with 2-4 ports and track demand. Hotels typically need chargers for 15-25% of parking spots
Consider including charging in the room rate rather than separate billing
Wall-mounted chargers near building entrances are cheaper to install than pedestal units
Power sharing across 4-6 chargers on one circuit keeps costs low for overnight charging

rules.name: Highway Corridor DC Fast Charging
rules.reasoning: Highway corridor charging requires 150 kW minimum, ideally 350 kW. At 350 kW, compatible vehicles add about 300 km of range in 10-15 minutes. This segment has the highest infrastructure cost but also the most government incentive support through programs like NEVI.
tips: NEVI grants can cover 80% of costs for qualifying highway corridor sites
Plan for at least 4 ports per site - NEVI minimum
Modular charger platforms let you add power capacity as demand grows
Site selection is critical - rest stops and fast food locations near highway exits see highest utilization
Budget for potential utility service upgrades - highway sites often need new transformer installations

rules.name: Transit / Public Transport Charging
rules.reasoning: Transit electrification typically involves large-battery vehicles (buses at 200-600 kWh, school buses at 100-200 kWh) with predictable routes and return-to-depot schedules. Overnight depot charging with managed Level 2 or medium-power DC (50-150 kW) is the most cost-effective approach. On-route opportunity charging at terminals using 150-450 kW chargers can supplement depot charging for routes too long for a single charge.
tips: Start with routes that are shortest and most predictable - these have the lowest risk
Depot overnight charging at 50-100 kW is 3-5x cheaper per bus than 450 kW en-route chargers
Federal Transit Administration (FTA) Low-No Emission grants cover buses and charging infrastructure
School bus electrification qualifies for EPA Clean School Bus rebates ($250K-375K per bus)
Vehicle-to-grid (V2G) with electric school buses can generate revenue during summer months
Plan electrical infrastructure for full fleet electrification from day one, even if starting with a pilot

rules.name: General EV Charging Recommendation
rules.reasoning: Level 2 charging covers 90%+ of charging needs at a fraction of the cost of DC Fast Charging. It is simpler to install, cheaper to operate, and usually sufficient when vehicles have more than 2 hours of dwell time. Start with Level 2, collect utilization data, and add DC Fast Charging only if dwell times are consistently too short.
tips: Start small and scale based on real data, not projections
Always choose OCPP-compatible chargers to avoid vendor lock-in and ensure future interoperability
Load management pays for itself on any deployment with 4+ ports
Check your local incentive programs - they can cover 50-80% of costs
Get at least 3 installation quotes from electricians with EV charger experience
OCPP 2.0.1 adds smart charging, ISO 15118 plug-and-charge, and improved security over OCPP 1.6J

rules.name: Municipal / Public Infrastructure Charging
rules.reasoning: Municipal charging serves the public and requires a different approach than commercial deployments. ADA accessibility compliance is mandatory for public installations. A mix of Level 2 and DCFC typically works best: Level 2 for library and park visitors with longer dwell times, DCFC for downtown locations where turnover matters. Federal NEVI and state/provincial grants often cover 80%+ of costs for public-access chargers. Signage, wayfinding, and integration with payment apps are essential for public adoption.
tips: ADA compliance is mandatory: at least one accessible charging space per site with proper signage and approach
NEVI grants require 4 DCFC ports minimum with 150 kW each at qualifying highway corridor sites
For city lots and parks, Level 2 with 4-8 hour dwell time is usually the best fit and lowest cost
Install clear wayfinding signage from main roads to charger locations
OCPP compliance is essential for municipal deployments to avoid vendor lock-in on multi-year contracts
Consider pricing that covers electricity and maintenance costs while remaining accessible to the public
Budget for ongoing network management fees and cellular connectivity costs in your operating budget
